name: High Value Exposure Source Candidates
description: Dry-run source-atlas candidates focused on sellable exposure monitoring: company mentions, actor-page changes, phishing/brand abuse, malware infrastructure, breach/stealer-log domain exposure, and direct ransomware verification. Importing this file must not start crawling or activate sources.

sources.name: urlscan.io public and account search
sources.legalNotes: Use only approved API access and visibility modes. Do not submit customer-private URLs as public scans. Store metadata, result links, screenshot hashes, domains, IPs, ASNs, page titles, verdicts, and first-seen timestamps.
sources.found: urlscan documents APIs for URL submissions, scan results, and search over existing scans by attributes such as domains, IPs, ASNs, and hashes.
gives: new phishing and impersonation pages for watched brands/domains
rendered-page metadata, result links, screenshot/page hashes, domains, IPs, ASNs, and first-seen timing
corroboration for actor infrastructure and customer-domain exposure
sources.whyWorthPaying: A startup can turn public/urlscan-visible web observations into customer-specific alerts: 'your brand/domain appeared in a suspicious scan today', clustered with hosting, cert, redirect, and page metadata. The buyer pays for speed, filtering, and watchlist matching, not for the raw urlscan API.
activationNextSteps: terms and quota review
build watchlist query canary for 25 owned domains
store only metadata and urlscan result URLs
dedupe by submitted URL hash, final domain, page hash, and screenshot hash

sources.name: Have I Been Pwned domain breach and stealer-log metadata
sources.legalNotes: Requires subscription/API key and domain ownership verification where applicable. Store breach names, data classes, breach dates, stealer-log domain metadata, and counts only. Do not store passwords, raw emails beyond customer-authorized scope, or credential values.
sources.found: HIBP v3 documents domain search, breach metadata, and stealer-log endpoints including website-domain and email-domain stealer-log lookups.
gives: verified breach metadata for customer domains
stealer-log domain exposure metadata without credential values
data classes, dates, breach names, and affected-domain context
sources.whyWorthPaying: Security teams pay when a monitored corporate domain or vendor domain shows fresh exposure and they get a clean notification with affected service/domain context. HIBP supplies trusted raw metadata; the sellable layer is domain authorization, triage, correlation with ransomware/actor activity, and clean customer workflow.
activationNextSteps: confirm subscription and permitted use
implement domain-verification workflow
store counts and metadata only
route sensitive results to customer-specific authenticated views

sources.name: ThreatFox recent IOC API
sources.legalNotes: Use Auth-Key and terms review. Recent IOCs only for defensive enrichment and correlation. Do not imply victim compromise from an IOC match alone.
sources.found: ThreatFox documents recent IOC queries, IOC search, malware-family queries, tag queries, and IOC expiration after six months from the API/export surface.
gives: malware-family and tag pivots for actor/campaign enrichment
freshness-limited infrastructure context for customer alerts
sources.whyWorthPaying: ThreatFox is not a unique paid row source, but it makes company alerts more useful: when a ransomware claim or phishing page appears, we can attach fresh malware/infrastructure context and avoid stale IOC noise.
activationNextSteps: terms and commercial API review
build seven-day IOC freshness canary
attach only to actor/company alerts as enrichment
suppress expired or stale indicators

sources.name: URLhaus active malware URL metadata
sources.legalNotes: Use URLhaus metadata and feeds for defensive monitoring only. Never download payloads. Respect fair-use/commercial API terms and minimum fetch intervals.
sources.found: URLhaus documents API and feed access for malware URLs, active malware distribution sites, RPZ hostnames, IDS rules, and recent/active database dumps with frequent generation.
gives: active malware distribution URL and hostname metadata
recent/active infrastructure signals for domains and hosts
malware-family and payload-reference context without downloading payloads
sources.whyWorthPaying: For a monitored company, this is valuable when its domains, vendors, or lookalikes appear in active malware infrastructure. The startup value is matching, dedupe, freshness scoring, and routing only relevant active signals into alerts.
activationNextSteps: commercial terms review
metadata-only adapter that blocks payload endpoints
freshness gate for active or recently-added records
dedupe by normalized URL, host, and URLhaus ID

sources.name: ransomware.live victim/group/infostealer seed plus direct verification
sources.legalNotes: Use as seed and corroboration. Do not resell unchanged public rows as final product. Direct verification must remain metadata-only and must not fetch leaked files, negotiation material, credentials, or payloads.
sources.found: ransomware.live presents victim, group, search, notification, statistics, and infostealer-oriented surfaces for ransomware monitoring.
gives: actor names, victim names, claimed dates, sectors/countries where available
seed URLs and actor-page candidates for direct metadata verification
infostealer-oriented public context for later authorized exposure monitoring
sources.whyWorthPaying: Everyone can index public ransomware.live rows; the sellable layer is using it to discover what to verify directly, alert customers faster, detect claim changes, and attach company/vendor watchlist matching.
activationNextSteps: keep public rows as seed/corroboration
derive actor-page candidates into restricted metadata review
store direct verification timestamps and page-change summaries
separate public-index source from owned direct-check source

sources.name: Certificate Transparency brand and domain watch
sources.legalNotes: Monitor public certificate transparency data for owned domains, vendor domains, and brand-like names. Do not claim compromise from a certificate alone; route as suspicious domain/brand drift.
sources.found: Certificate Transparency logs expose newly issued certificates that can be searched for domains and brand-like names through public CT search surfaces such as crt.sh.
gives: brand/domain drift before phishing pages are reported elsewhere
entity-resolution pivots for urlscan, URLhaus, and customer watchlists
sources.whyWorthPaying: Customers pay for early warning on suspicious domain and certificate creation tied to their brand, executives, products, or vendors. The raw logs are public; the product value is matching, scoring, and correlating with phishing/malware/ransomware signals.
activationNextSteps: pick approved CT query backend
build fuzzy brand matching with false-positive controls
correlate with urlscan and URLhaus before high-severity alerting
dedupe by certificate fingerprint and normalized name
